title: Brads cheat sheet
summary: Master exam-thinking cheat sheet: how to dissect any question, the keyword→answer map, the high-yield traps across every domain, and quick-reference theory, formulas, tools and process rules.

sections.heading: How to dissect any question (do this every time)
sections.body:
Run this checklist on every question:

- Read the LAST sentence first. What is it actually asking — first, next, best, or most important? The call word changes the answer.
- Classify the situation: predictive / agile / hybrid? planning / executing / monitoring & controlling / closing? a current problem (issue) or a future uncertainty (risk)?
- Scan for keyword triggers (next section).
- Eliminate aggressively: bypassing process (especially a baseline), jumping to escalate/remove-someone, the PM deciding for an agile team, or any passive 'just log/monitor/inform' when action is needed.
- Choose the option that is proactive, follows the correct process/order, and gives authority to the right role.
sections.tip:
'First / next' rewards the correct process order and the least-drastic correct action.
'Best / most appropriate' rewards the option that fixes the root cause through proper process.

sections.heading: Agile vs predictive vs hybrid — read the signals
sections.body:
The delivery approach tells you the flavour of the right answer.

- Agile signals: self-organising team, sprint, iteration, user story, backlog, Product Owner, daily standup. Correct flavour — empower the team, diagnose WITH the team, support the PO.
- Predictive signals: baseline, WBS, change request, CCB, critical path, earned value. Correct flavour — follow formal process, submit a change request.
- Hybrid signals: mixes both, or mentions tailoring. Correct flavour — fit the tool to each part; for unclear requirements, break down and prioritise the KNOWN ones into iterations rather than waiting.
sections.tip:
Sprints / backlog / PO → Agile.
Baselines / WBS / CCB / critical path → Predictive.
Mixes both or 'tailoring' → Hybrid.

sections.heading: The keyword → move map (memorise this)
sections.body:
A large share of the exam is pattern-matching these triggers:

- 'Baseline' / 'approved schedule/cost/scope' → raise a Change Request.
- 'Has happened / is happening / was pulled / is delaying' → Issue Log.
- 'May / could / potential / in the future' → Risk Register.
- 'New stakeholder / new role appeared' → update the Stakeholder Engagement Plan.
- 'Team is struggling / missing milestones' → diagnose WITH the team before changing timelines or adding rewards.
- 'Value is in doubt / long timeline / may not meet needs' → prototype or MVP.
- 'Functional manager pulled the resource' → talk to the functional manager FIRST.
- 'Don't know current vs desired engagement' → Stakeholder Engagement Assessment Matrix.
- 'Stakeholders not attending' → reach out to them individually.
- 'Delays implementing risk responses' → missing or unclear Risk Owners.
- 'Align projects with business objectives across the org' → PMO.
- 'High-risk user stories' → schedule them in EARLIER iterations.
sections.tip:
Drill these until they're reflexes.
On a close question, find the trigger phrase first — it usually decides the answer.

sections.heading: Issue vs Risk — now vs later
sections.body:
The single most repeated distinction. Ask: has it happened yet?

- Issue = a current, realised problem (it is happening now) → Issue Log.
- Risk = a future uncertain event (it might happen) → Risk Register.

The classic trap is treating a current, realised event as a future risk. A quality defect that has already appeared is an issue to solve, not a risk to log.
sections.tip:
'Has it happened?'
Yes → Issue Log.
No / uncertain → Risk Register.

sections.heading: Plan vs Register — the document trap
sections.body:
Distractors constantly swap the Plan and the Register, for both risk and stakeholders.

- Risk Management Plan = HOW risk will be run (methodology, roles, thresholds); made once.
- Stakeholder Engagement Plan = HOW you'll engage each stakeholder; update it when roles or strategies change.
- Risk Register = the living list of risks, owners and responses; update it for a specific risk.
- Stakeholder Register = the people, roles, interest/influence; update it for a new person or detail.

When the engagement STRATEGY or risk APPROACH changes, you update the Plan — not just the Register.
sections.tip:
Strategy / approach / how-we-engage → the PLAN.
A specific item or person → the REGISTER.

sections.heading: Risk management — process & responses
sections.body:
Know the process order cold:

- 1. Plan Risk Management — defines HOW risk will be managed.
- 2. Identify Risks — create the Risk Register (high-level first), performed throughout the project.
- 3. Perform Qualitative Risk Analysis — prioritise by probability × impact.
- 4. Perform Quantitative Risk Analysis — model the numeric effect (not always done).
- 5. Plan Risk Responses — develop the response actions (the 'risk action plan').
- 6. Implement Risk Responses — owners carry them out.
- 7. Monitor Risks — track residual/secondary risks, watch for new ones, confirm responses worked.

Response strategies:

- Threats: Escalate, Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept.
- Opportunities: Escalate, Exploit, Share, Enhance, Accept.

Identify before you respond; for a new worry, identify high-level risks first (don't quantify yet); every response gets a Risk Owner who implements it.
sections.tip:
New risk situation → identify/assess first, never jump to a number or a solution.
'Risk action plan' = Plan Risk Responses, not the overall Risk Management Plan.

sections.heading: More risk traps
sections.body:
The nuances that catch people:

- A newly identified risk during execution → first DOCUMENT it in the risk register, then analyse/respond.
- A known risk occurs and you pick the planned response → update the risk register with the new status.
- A planned-for risk fires (contingency exists) → consult the risk response plan / contract SOW first; don't invent a new response.
- A known risk's planned response is now ineffective and a new one is urgent → update the risk management plan and implement the new response.
- After implementing a response → confirm its effectiveness via Monitor Risks (a fix can backfire).
- A risk beyond your authority or the project's scope (e.g. an uninvolved senior leader) → Escalate.
- Mitigate vs Transfer: mitigate = reduce probability/impact (build redundancy, add QA); transfer = shift the impact AND ownership to a third party (insurance, contract, specialist firm).
- Risk categories:
  - Technical (scope, requirements, a missing Definition of Done).
  - External (regulation, market, weather).
  - Management/Organisational (resourcing, processes).
  - Commercial (contracts, procurement).
- Risk attitudes: risk-averse, risk-neutral, risk-tolerant, risk-seeking — these set thresholds (not high/medium/low, and not the response strategies).
sections.tip:
New risk → log it in the register first.
Planned-for risk fires → consult the response plan, don't improvise.
Reduce it = mitigate; hand it off with ownership = transfer.

sections.heading: Risk numbers & reserves
sections.body:
Often-confused risk concepts:

- EMV (Expected Monetary Value) = Probability × Impact; sum identified risks to size contingency.
- Contingency reserve = for KNOWN (identified) risks; sits INSIDE the cost/schedule baseline; the PM controls it.
- Management reserve = for UNKNOWN (unforeseen) risks; sits OUTSIDE the baseline; needs management approval to release.
- Secondary risk = a new risk created BY a response you implemented.
- Residual risk = the risk that REMAINS after a response.
sections.tip:
Known risk → contingency reserve (in the baseline, PM controls).
Unknown / unforeseen risk → management reserve (outside the baseline, needs approval).

sections.heading: Change control & baselines
sections.body:
Once scope, schedule or cost is baselined you cannot edit it directly. Any baseline change runs through Perform Integrated Change Control: raise a Change Request, assess impact, the CCB decides, then update.

These documents update freely WITHOUT a change request:

- Stakeholder Register.
- Risk Register.
- Issue Log.
- Assumption Log.
- Lessons Learned Register.

Even a 'free' enhancement with no cost or schedule impact must go through change control and be documented. An incentive added to a fixed/lump-sum contract updates the cost baseline.
sections.tip:
'Update / revise the baseline' → Change Request, almost every time.
Logs and registers are fair game to update without one.

sections.heading: Scope, requirements & integrated change
sections.body:
How scope is built and protected:

- The project charter holds the high-level scope and the success criteria, and is the starting point for the WBS — approve the charter before detailed planning.
- Build the WBS WITH the team by decomposing the approved scope into work packages; a good WBS is deliverable-oriented, hierarchical and obeys the 100% rule (it's an internal tool, not a customer document).
- Change-request types:
  - corrective action (fix something off-track).
  - preventive action (stop a FUTURE problem).
  - defect repair (fix a defect).
  - updates.
- Customer requirement = general, in the customer's words ('easy to use'); technical requirement = specific and testable. You translate customer needs into technical ones.
- A missing feature found at acceptance → the gap was in scope definition / the scope management plan.
- A post-acceptance complaint that requirements weren't met → review the Validate Scope process to find the root cause.
sections.tip:
Charter → WBS (with the team) → detailed requirements.
Every change goes through Integrated Change Control, even a 'free' one.

sections.heading: The seven quality tools — which to reach for
sections.body:
Pick the tool by what the question asks:

- Root cause of a problem → cause-and-effect / fishbone / Ishikawa diagram (one tool, three names).
- Rank the 'vital few' causes to fix first (80/20) → Pareto chart.
- Distribution or count of defects → histogram.
- Is the process stable / in control? → control chart.
- Relationship between two variables → scatter diagram.
- Verify a task's steps were all done, or ensure consistency → checklist (check sheet).
- Map the steps of a process → flowchart.
- A trend over time → run chart.
- Test a small improvement then standardise it → Deming's PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle.
sections.tip:
Cause → fishbone.
Prioritise the few → Pareto.
Stable? → control chart.
Verify steps → checklist.

sections.heading: Quality processes & cost of quality
sections.body:
Don't mix the three quality processes:

- Plan Quality Management → decide the standards and how you'll meet them (early).
- Manage Quality (≈ quality assurance) → make sure the right PROCESSES are used; audits, prevention, process improvement. A quality audit = Manage Quality.
- Control Quality → inspect the DELIVERABLES to find defects; statistical sampling is a tool here.

Then Validate Scope = the CUSTOMER formally accepts the deliverables (Control Quality comes first, internally).

Cost of quality:

- Conformance = prevention + appraisal (building it right and checking).
- Nonconformance = internal + external failure (fixing or escaping defects). Prevention is cheaper than failure.

Control limits are the process's own voice (±3σ, set by the process); specification limits come from the customer. Quality is built in and continuously improved, not inspected in — don't cut a healthy QA budget just because quality looks fine.
sections.tip:
Audit / process focus = Manage Quality.
Inspect deliverables for defects = Control Quality.
Customer signs off = Validate Scope.

sections.heading: Roles & authority
sections.body:
Keep these straight — the exam confuses them on purpose:

- Project Manager — delivers day to day; in agile, facilitates and removes impediments.
- Sponsor — funds and champions ONE project, owns the business case, removes high-level obstacles.
- PMO — sets standards and governance, and can be a key decision-maker keeping projects aligned to business objectives ACROSS the organisation.
- Product Owner — owns the product, the backlog and its priority, and what gets released.
- Functional Manager — owns the people/resources in a matrix org.

Program = related projects managed together for benefits you can't get separately. Portfolio = all work aligned to strategy.
sections.tip:
'Aligns/standardises across projects' = PMO.
'Funds & champions THIS project' = Sponsor.
Confusing the two is the classic trap.

sections.heading: PM authority by org structure
sections.body:
How much power the PM has depends on the structure:

- Functional — PM has little/no authority; the functional manager runs the show.
- Weak matrix — PM is a coordinator/expediter; limited authority.
- Balanced matrix — authority shared between PM and functional manager.
- Strong matrix — PM has moderate-to-high authority; resources still report functionally.
- Projectized — PM has high/full authority; the team reports to the PM.
sections.tip: More 'matrix/functional' in the scenario → the PM must NEGOTIATE with functional managers for people, not direct them.

sections.heading: Leadership styles
sections.body:
- Directive (Directing) — task and results focused; tells the team what and how. Good for crises, inexperienced teams, or when structure is needed.
- Servant leadership — serves the team, removes impediments, builds the environment. The agile default, and the right move when moving from predictive to hybrid.
- Situational — adapt the style to the team's maturity: Directing → Coaching → Supporting → Delegating.
- Transactional — motivates through rewards and penalties.
- Transformational — inspires through vision and growth.
- Laissez-faire — hands-off; suits a highly capable, self-directed team.
sections.tip:
Agile / self-organising team → servant leadership.
Crisis or brand-new team → directive.
Match the style to the team's maturity.

sections.heading: Motivation theories
sections.body:
High-yield and easily confused:

- Maslow — hierarchy of needs (physiological → safety → social → esteem → self-actualisation); lower needs first.
- Herzberg — hygiene factors (salary, conditions) only PREVENT dissatisfaction; motivators (achievement, recognition, growth) drive satisfaction.
- McGregor — Theory X (people dislike work, need control) vs Theory Y (self-motivated, seek responsibility).
- McClelland — needs for Achievement, Affiliation, Power.
- Vroom's Expectancy — effort → performance → a reward the person actually values.
- Ouchi — Theory Z: long-term employment and loyalty drive productivity.
sections.tip:
'Effort leads to a reward they value' = Expectancy.
'Pay/conditions don't motivate, only stop dissatisfaction' = Herzberg.

sections.heading: Conflict, ground rules & team issues
sections.body:
Match the intervention to the problem:

- One person underperforming or demotivated → a private one-on-one to find the root cause; don't reassign work or tell the whole team to 'focus more'.
- A whole-team problem (low interaction, recurring complaint) → bring the team together or confidentially poll for the root cause; incentives usually backfire.
- Strong emotions in a conflict → acknowledge and address the emotions FIRST (emotional intelligence) before problem-solving; never assign blame.
- Ground rules ignored → you (the servant leader) reinforce them with the team; it's not HR's, the functional manager's, or the sponsor's job. Address a repeat offender privately.
- New members struggling to learn from seniors → facilitate peer mentoring.
- Cross-cultural friction → revisit the team charter (values/working agreements) together; build cultural awareness before issuing directives.

Conflict modes (Thomas-Kilmann):

- Collaborate / problem-solve — confront the issue together for a win/win. The PMP-preferred mode.
- Compromise — both give something up (lose/lose).
- Smooth / accommodate — emphasise agreement.
- Force / direct — impose a solution (win/lose); emergencies only.
- Withdraw / avoid — retreat; trivial issues only.

Tuckman's stages: Forming → Storming → Norming → Performing → Adjourning.
sections.tip:
Individual issue → one-on-one.
Team issue → the team.
Emotions → address them first.
Ground rules → you reinforce them, don't escalate.
Conflict → collaborate/confront the root cause.

sections.heading: Stakeholder engagement
sections.body:
- Plan vs Register: update the Engagement Plan when roles or strategies change; the Register holds the who.
- Stakeholder Engagement Assessment Matrix — maps each stakeholder's CURRENT vs DESIRED engagement (Unaware → Resistant → Neutral → Supportive → Leading). Use it when you don't know the gap.
- Power/Interest grid — high/high = Manage Closely; high power/low interest = Keep Satisfied; low power/high interest = Keep Informed; low/low = Monitor.
- Salience model — Power, Legitimacy, Urgency (all three = Definitive, top priority).
- Stakeholders not attending → reach out individually to understand why; don't just escalate or cancel.
- A busy stakeholder questioning frequent reviews → explain the value (less rework) before negotiating reduced attendance.
sections.tip: Engagement is tailored per stakeholder — and that tailoring lives in the PLAN, not the register.

sections.heading: Communications
sections.body:
- Tailor communication to each stakeholder: who needs it, what, why, when, how often, by what method, in what context.
- Lead decision-makers with business VALUE, not technical detail buried in an appendix.
- Don't keep using one fixed method — reassess and adapt the style to the audience.
- Methods: interactive (meetings/calls — best for complex topics), push (emails/reports — sent out), pull (portals/wikis — stakeholders fetch it).
sections.tip:
Tailor the message and method to each stakeholder.
Decision-makers want value, not pages of equations.

sections.heading: Procurement — documents, contracts & phases
sections.body:
Bid documents:

- RFI (Request for Information) → gather information, narrow the field.
- RFQ (Request for Quote) → get a price for well-defined goods.
- RFP (Request for Proposal) → complex need, solution not obvious; the most formal, with full requirements and acceptance criteria. A new/replacement vendor needs the RFP.

Contract types and who carries the risk:

- Fixed Price — seller carries the most risk.
  - FFP.
  - FPIF.
  - FPEPA for long-term/international deals with inflation or currency risk.
- Cost-Reimbursable — buyer carries the most risk.
  - CPFF.
  - CPIF.
  - CPAF.
- Time & Material — medium risk; good for staff augmentation or small/uncertain scope.

Phases:

- Plan Procurements → make-or-buy, decide what to buy.
- Conduct Procurements → issue RFx, evaluate, select, award.
- Control Procurements → monitor and evaluate vendor performance.
- Close Procurements → verify completion, finalise and pay.

The Statement of Work (SOW) defines the work to be done — check delivered work against it. Transfer a specific risk → insurance or a contract clause.
sections.tip:
Complex need / unknown solution → RFP.
Well-defined scope → Fixed Price; unclear scope → Cost-Reimbursable or T&M.
Monitoring a vendor = Control Procurements; paying them off = Close Procurements.

sections.heading: Schedule
sections.body:
- Critical path = the LONGEST path; it has zero float and drives the duration. Two independent paths → the critical one is the longer.
- Total float = slip without delaying the project; free float = without delaying the successor. Critical-path activities have zero total float.
- Crashing = add resources to the critical path (costs money). Fast-tracking = run sequential work in parallel (adds risk/rework). Both only help on the critical path.
- Leads overlap a successor; lags insert a wait.
- Resource leveling can extend the schedule; smoothing stays within float.
- Never cut planned tests just to gain float — that trades quality for time.
sections.tip:
Two paths → critical = longest.
Crash = money, fast-track = risk; only the critical path matters.

sections.heading: Estimating & earned value
sections.body:
Estimating methods:

- Analogous — top-down from a similar past project; fast, cheap, least accurate.
- Parametric — rate × quantity; accurate with good data.
- Bottom-up — sum the detailed work packages; MOST accurate, most effort.
- Three-point (PERT) — tE = (O + 4M + P) / 6; range ≈ (P − O) / 6.

Earned value (memorise the signs):

- SV = EV − PV; CV = EV − AC. Negative = behind / over.
- SPI = EV / PV; CPI = EV / AC. Below 1 = behind / over.
- EAC = BAC / CPI (current rate continues); VAC = BAC − EAC.

Judgement calls:

- CPI below 1 (over budget) → be proactive: develop a bottom-up estimate of the remaining work (not contingency, parallelism or a change request first).
- Benefit-cost ratio (BCR) below 1 → costs outweigh benefits → consult the sponsor on feasibility (above 1 → benefits win).
sections.tip:
Variance = EV minus (PV or AC); index = EV over (PV or AC); below 1 is bad.
CPI < 1 → bottom-up estimate the rest.
BCR < 1 → costs exceed benefits → ask the sponsor.

sections.heading: Closure & transition
sections.body:
Closure has an order, and it's tested:

- Confirm deliverables are accepted → close out contracts/procurement → capture lessons learned → release resources and disband the team (last).
- Don't close the project until finance confirms all payments are correct.
- Lessons learned: analyse and organise the findings WITH stakeholders to extract insight, then store them in the lessons-learned register (an organisational process asset).
- A cancelled project (even during initiation) → follow the organisation's closure guidelines.
- Transition to operations/BAU → formal handover of accountability to the ops team, plus formal training and documentation for users.
- A deliverable not accepted near closure → raise a Change Request (defect repair) to the CCB.
- Project success / closure criteria → defined in the project charter; if they're not met, analyse WHY first.
sections.tip:
Closure order: accept → close contracts → lessons learned → release the team.
Lessons learned live in the register (an OPA).

sections.heading: Common wrong-answer patterns — eliminate fast
sections.body:
On a close call, strike out options that:

- Bypass formal process — especially editing a baseline without a Change Request.
- Have the PM deciding for a self-organising agile team.
- Jump straight to 'escalate to the sponsor' or 'remove/replace the person'.
- Are passive ('just inform', 'just monitor', 'just log it') when action is needed.
- Treat a current, realised problem as a future risk.
- Update the wrong artifact (the Register when it should be the Plan).
- Ignore the correct process order, or quantify before identifying.
- Assume the team needs rewards when the real issue is support, skills or conflict.
sections.tip: If an option does any of these, it's probably the distractor — even if it sounds reasonable.

sections.heading: When in doubt — the mindset table
sections.body:
- Baseline needs changing → Change Request (not modify directly).
- Current problem → Issue Log (not Risk Register).
- Future uncertainty → Risk Register (not Issue Log).
- New risk spotted → log it in the register first, then act.
- Agile team struggling → diagnose WITH the team (not PM decides / change the timeline).
- One person struggling → a one-on-one (not a team-wide fix).
- Value questioned → prototype / MVP (not extend the timeline).
- Resource pulled by a functional manager → talk to the functional manager first.
- New stakeholder / role → update the Stakeholder Engagement Plan.
- Organisation-wide alignment → PMO (not the Sponsor).
- High-risk items in agile → address EARLIER.
- Conflict → collaborate/confront the root cause.
sections.tip:
These cover a large fraction of the exam.
If you can recall the left column from the right-hand cue, you'll bank easy marks.

sections.heading: Exam-day reminders
sections.body:
- Read the LAST sentence first, then the scenario.
- 'First / next / best' → think process order and the least-drastic correct action.
- Agile/hybrid → favour empowerment, team diagnosis and Product Owner authority.
- Predictive → favour formal process and Change Requests for baselines.
- Eliminate answers that are passive, skip process, or put the wrong person in charge.
- Manage the clock — flag and move on; don't sink five minutes into one item.
- Trust the PMP mindset: proactive, servant-leader, value-driven, process-respecting.
sections.tip: Most ties break the same way: the proactive option that follows process and empowers the right role wins.

source: Personal master cheat sheet (PMBOK 7 focus) + practice-exam trap analysis
